headline: Jeannette Rankin, First Woman in Congress, Born, 1880
body: On December 9, 1880, Jeannette Pickering Rankin, a pioneering figure in American politics and women's rights, was born near Missoula, Montana Territory. Rankin made history in 1916 when she became the first woman ever elected to the U.S. Congress, representing Montana. A staunch pacifist, she was one of only 50 members of Congress to vote against entering World War I in 1917, and the sole member of Congress to vote against entering World War II in 1941. Her political career was marked by her unwavering commitment to peace and social justice, advocating for women's suffrage, child welfare, and workers' rights. Rankin's legacy extends far beyond Montana, as she paved the way for women in politics and remained a vocal advocate for her principles throughout her life. Her birth in Montana set the stage for a remarkable career that challenged norms and championed progressive causes on a national and international scale.
